items.q: What is characteristic of Baroque music?
items.correct: Continuous bass line, ornamentation, terraced dynamics, and counterpoint.
items.why: Students project later styles backwards. Baroque (1600-1750) used basso continuo, harpsichord/organ, and terraced (sudden) dynamics. Piano hadn't yet replaced harpsichord.

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.
Correct answer: Melody + chord symbols. “Full SATB” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A recurring motif identifying a character is a:
items.why: It's Leitmotif. “Cadenza” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many voices does a standard SATB chorale have?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
